when_to_use: Many of English's most common verbs do NOT take -ed in the past — they change form completely (go→went, have→had, see→saw). These must be memorized individually; there is no spelling rule.
when_to_use_ar: كتير من أكثر الأفعال الإنجليزية استخدامًا مش بتاخد -ed في الماضي — بتتغير شكلها بالكامل (go→went, have→had, see→saw). لازم تحفظها واحدة واحدة، مفيش قاعدة إملائية.
why_arabic_speakers_struggle: Learners who just mastered the -ed rule in Week 1 often over-apply it here ('goed', 'haved', 'seed'), because it feels like a safe, learned pattern. Irregular verbs must be treated as separate vocabulary items to memorize, not as an extension of the -ed rule.

examples.en: I went to the market yesterday.
We ate at a new restaurant.

common_errors.explanation: 'go' is irregular — it becomes 'went', never 'goed'. There is no -ed rule to apply here.

quick_rule: There is no spelling shortcut for irregular verbs — the top 20 most common English verbs are almost all irregular (go, have, see, eat, come, do, make, take, get, know...). Learn 3-4 new ones per week alongside your regular vocabulary.
quick_rule_ar: مفيش قاعدة إملائية للأفعال الشاذة — أكتر ٢٠ فعل استخدامًا في الإنجليزية أغلبهم شاذين. احفظ ٣-٤ أفعال جديدة كل أسبوع مع باقي مفرداتك.
mnemonic: IRREGULAR = 'I Refuse to be REGULAR' — think of these verbs as rebels that broke the -ed rule on purpose.
connect_to_speaking: When you tell a story about your day, you'll almost always need at least 2-3 irregular verbs (went, had, saw, ate, did). Practice: tell your buddy your full routine from yesterday using only irregular past verbs where needed — no 'goed' or 'haved' allowed!
